heb.paragraph: "Let the LORD judge (shōfēṭ) the peoples. Vindicate me, LORD, according to my righteousness" (v.8). David appeals to God as judge. His defence: integrity. His request: fair trial. The psalm combines lament with legal vocabulary — the courtroom in prayer.

refs.note: "Vindicate me, LORD, for I have led a blameless life" — the same innocence claim.
"Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord" — the principle David enacts by appealing to God rather than retaliating.

notes.note: "LORD my God, I take refuge in you; save and deliver me from all who pursue me." Refuge is the first word; rescue is the request.

"If I have done this... let my enemy pursue me and overtake me." The self-imprecation: David invites destruction if he is guilty. This is the oath of innocence — the same legal instrument Job used (Job 31).
"Vindicate me, LORD, according to my righteousness." David appeals to divine justice, not divine mercy. He believes his case will stand.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the self-imprecation is conditionally structured: "if I have done this... then let the enemy..." The grammar mirrors legal oaths.
Calvin: David appeals to righteousness, not sinlessness. Calvin: in this specific case, he is innocent. He does not claim universal perfection.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "vindicate me" (shāfṭēnî) is a judicial verb: declare me righteous in this case. It is forensic, not moral.
The NET: the oath formula "if I have done X, let Y happen" is the standard ANE self-imprecation — the defendant stakes their life on their innocence.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the self-imprecation is the strongest form of innocence claim. David invites his own destruction if he is lying. This is not arrogance but legal confidence.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the courtroom imagery pervades this psalm. God is judge, David is defendant, the enemies are prosecutors. The verdict is requested.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "arise, LORD, in your anger" — the judicial vocabulary: God rises from the bench to deliver the verdict. The judge stands to act.

hist.context: David asks for rescue from pursuers (vv.1–2), protests his innocence with a self-imprecation (vv.3–5: if I have done wrong, let the enemy destroy me), and calls God to arise in anger against the rage of enemies (vv.6–7). The appeal: judge me according to my righteousness and integrity (vv.8–9). Like Job, David demands a fair hearing.

heb.paragraph: "Whoever digs a hole (shaḥat) and scoops it out falls into the pit they have made" (v.15). The boomerang principle: evil returns to its sender. The one who digs a trap falls into it. The mischief intended for others lands on the schemer’s own head. Justice is built into the moral structure of reality.

refs.note: "Whoever digs a pit will fall into it" — the same boomerang proverb.
Haman hanged on the gallows he built for Mordecai — the narrative enactment of the principle.
"A man reaps what he sows" — Paul’s statement of the same moral law.

notes.note: "God is a righteous judge, a God who displays his wrath every day. If he does not relent, he will sharpen his sword." Divine patience has a limit. The imagery is military: God arms himself against the unrepentant.
"Whoever is pregnant with evil conceives trouble and gives birth to disillusionment. They dig a pit and fall into it." Evil’s self-destructive nature. The boomerang principle.
"I will give thanks to the LORD because of his righteousness." The psalm ends in worship, not vengeance. David’s trust in divine justice produces gratitude.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the pregnancy-to-birth metaphor tracks evil through its full gestation. The mother of evil is also its victim.
Calvin: the boomerang principle is God’s active justice, not mere karma. Calvin: God directs the recoil. The pit does not merely exist; God ensures the digger falls in.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: shaḥat (pit) is the same word used in Proverbs 26:27. The wisdom tradition and the psalm tradition share this justice metaphor.
The NET: the psalm closes with praise — the expected resolution of a lament. The structure: plea → trust → praise.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the pregnancy metaphor is vivid: evil is conceived, carried, and delivered — but the birth produces disillusionment, not triumph. The schemer is the first victim.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the boomerang principle is not magical but theological: God ensures that evil recoils. The moral universe is not neutral but actively just.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the pit image is one of the Psalter’s most common justice images. The trap-setter is caught in their own trap. God’s justice uses the enemy’s own weapons.

hist.context: God is described as a righteous judge who displays wrath every day (v.11). If the wicked do not repent, God sharpens his sword, bends his bow, prepares deadly weapons (vv.12–13). Then the boomerang: the wicked conceive trouble, become pregnant with mischief, give birth to disillusionment (v.14). They dig a pit and fall into it (v.15). Their violence returns on their own heads (v.16). David closes with thanksgiving: "I will give thanks to the LORD because of his righteousness" (v.17).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVArise, Lord, in your anger; rise up against the rage of my enemies. Awake, my God; decree justice.ESVArise, O LORD, in your anger; lift yourself up against the fury of my enemies; awake for me; you have appointed a judgment.NIVthen let my enemy pursue and overtake me; let him trample my life to the ground and make me sleep in the dust.ESVlet the enemy pursue my soul and overtake it, and let him trample my life to the ground and lay my glory in the dust. Selah

rec.text: ""LORD my God, I take refuge in you; save and deliver me from all who pursue me." Refuge is the first word; rescue is the request."

rows.label: The Oath — 7:1–9
rows.text: Self-imprecation: "If I have done this, let me be destroyed." Vindicate me.

hebtext.note: "Let the LORD judge (shōfēṭ) the peoples. Vindicate me, LORD, according to my righteousness" (v.8). David appeals to God as judge. His defence: integrity. His request: fair trial. The psalm combines lament with legal vocabulary — the courtroom in prayer.

"Whoever digs a hole (shaḥat) and scoops it out falls into the pit they have made" (v.15). The boomerang principle: evil returns to its sender. The one who digs a trap falls into it. The mischief intended for others lands on the schemer’s own head. Justice is built into the moral structure of reality.

thread.text: "Vindicate me, LORD, for I have led a blameless life" — the same innocence claim.
"Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord" — the principle David enacts by appealing to God rather than retaliating.
"Whoever digs a pit will fall into it" — the same boomerang proverb.
Haman hanged on the gallows he built for Mordecai — the narrative enactment of the principle.
"A man reaps what he sows" — Paul’s statement of the same moral law.

themes.note: Justice peaks: courtroom plea + boomerang principle. Judgment: God as armed judge. Holiness: God’s wrath against unrepentant evil.
